heuristics.rule: Apply for OPT 60–90 days before your program completion date, not after graduation
heuristics.condition: When you are an F-1 student approaching the end of your degree program
heuristics.action: File I-765 with USCIS after DSO authorizes in SEVIS, targeting submission 60–90 days before academic completion
heuristics.because: USCIS processing takes 3–5 months; applying at or after graduation creates a work authorization gap during which you cannot legally work

heuristics.rule: Set your SEVIS transfer release date AFTER your OPT end date if you plan to continue OPT
heuristics.condition: When you are on post-completion OPT and considering transferring to a new school
heuristics.action: Delay setting the transfer release date in SEVIS until after your OPT authorization period ends
heuristics.because: OPT authorization terminates automatically the moment a transfer release date is set; there are zero exceptions and OPT cannot be transferred to the new school

heuristics.rule: Get your I-20 travel-endorsed by your DSO before every international departure during OPT
heuristics.condition: When you are on OPT and planning to travel outside the US
heuristics.action: Visit your DSO to obtain a travel signature on your I-20 (valid for 6 months) before leaving the country
heuristics.because: CBP will deny re-entry if the I-20 travel signature is missing or expired, regardless of your valid visa or EAD

heuristics.rule: Track your OPT unemployment days as a hard-count depleting balance starting from OPT start date
heuristics.condition: When you are on post-completion OPT and between jobs or waiting for USCIS processing
heuristics.action: Count every calendar day without authorized employment against your 90-day limit and alert your DSO when approaching 60 days
heuristics.because: Exceeding 90 cumulative unemployment days terminates OPT status automatically — USCIS does not send a warning before termination

heuristics.rule: Apply for STEM OPT extension before your current OPT EAD expires
heuristics.condition: When you hold a STEM degree, are employed by an E-Verify employer, and are in the final months of standard 12-month OPT
heuristics.action: Submit the STEM extension application (with I-983 Training Plan signed by employer) while current OPT is still valid
heuristics.because: Filing after OPT expiration means your work authorization has already lapsed; there is no grace period for late STEM extension filings

heuristics.rule: Identify your program completion date from academic records, not from the graduation ceremony calendar
heuristics.condition: When calculating the start of your F-1 60-day grace period
heuristics.action: Confirm with your registrar the exact date all academic requirements are fulfilled — this is the legal completion date
heuristics.because: Graduation ceremonies often occur months after actual academic completion; relying on ceremony date causes students to miscalculate the grace period and accrue unlawful presence

heuristics.rule: If planning to pursue a second degree after the first, preserve each degree level's independent OPT eligibility
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student is strategically considering enrollment in a new degree program to reset OPT eligibility
heuristics.action: Enroll in a degree at a different level (e.g., bachelor's → master's → PhD) to access a fresh 12-month OPT authorization
heuristics.because: Each degree level independently qualifies for its own 12-month OPT; this is a legal mechanism to extend total authorized work time in the US

decision_factors.factor: Program completion date vs. graduation ceremony date
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The 60-day grace period and OPT application window are triggered by academic completion, not the ceremony — conflating them causes students to miscalculate all downstream deadlines
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Request the official program completion date from the registrar; compare it to the graduation ceremony date on the academic calendar

decision_factors.factor: STEM degree eligibility for OPT extension
decision_factors.why_it_matters: STEM-eligible students can extend OPT by 24 months beyond the standard 12, dramatically increasing time to secure H-1B sponsorship or alternative status
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check whether your CIP code appears on the DHS STEM designated degree program list; confirm employer is enrolled in E-Verify

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Crossing 90 days (standard OPT) or 150 days (STEM extension) triggers automatic termination with no reinstatement option
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Maintain a personal log tracking every day without an active employer on record since OPT start date; count USCIS processing gaps if EAD was delayed

decision_factors.why_it_matters: STEM OPT extension is only available when employed by an E-Verify employer; a non-E-Verify employer disqualifies the student from STEM extension regardless of degree
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the HR or legal team directly and verify on the E-Verify employer search portal before accepting an offer

decision_factors.factor: Timing of planned international travel relative to I-20 travel signature validity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: An expired or missing travel signature on the I-20 results in CBP re-entry denial even with a valid visa and EAD
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the travel endorsement date on your I-20; it must be within the past 6 months at the time of re-entry

decision_factors.factor: School transfer timing relative to OPT end date
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Setting a transfer release date in SEVIS before OPT ends immediately terminates work authorization with no recovery path
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm current OPT EAD expiration date; only initiate transfer release after that date has passed

decision_factors.factor: Degree level already used for OPT
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Students who have already used OPT at a given degree level cannot reuse it at the same level; a new degree at a higher level unlocks fresh eligibility
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review SEVIS history or DSO records to identify which degree levels have had OPT authorized

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until graduation day to start the OPT application
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Creates a 3–5 month unauthorized work gap between graduation and EAD arrival; unemployment days may begin accumulating immediately against the 90-day limit even before the student can legally work
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who receive job offers contingent on immediate work authorization and students in competitive fields with early start date expectations

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Transferring to a new school while on post-completion OPT
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The act of setting the SEVIS transfer release date instantly and irrevocably terminates OPT work authorization — the student cannot undo this or continue OPT at the new school
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who believe their OPT will follow them to their new institution, particularly those mid-employment who don't consult a DSO before initiating the transfer

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on the 90-day unemployment limit as a buffer between jobs
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: USCIS processing delays, employer onboarding gaps, and the grace period itself all consume the same 90-day pool; what feels like ample buffer can vanish before the student has started a single job
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students changing employers, students whose OPT EAD was delayed, and students in industries with slow hiring cycles

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Traveling internationally during OPT without verifying I-20 travel signature
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Re-entry is denied at the port of entry regardless of visa validity or employment status; the student may be stranded abroad and lose their OPT authorization period
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who travel urgently (family emergencies, weddings) and do not have time to visit their DSO; students whose DSO office has long appointment wait times

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Enrolling in a second degree to reset OPT eligibility
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Extends time in the US legally but restarts the H-1B lottery exposure clock; the student may spend additional years and tuition cost only to face the same lottery odds without guaranteed sponsorship
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who treat degree enrollment as a pure immigration strategy without genuine academic or career rationale

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating the 60-day grace period as free time after graduation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Unemployment days on OPT accumulate during the grace period countdown; inaction on status decisions during this window may permanently foreclose options
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who do not understand the difference between a grace period (transition window) and a holiday; particularly affects students in December graduation cohorts who assume they have until spring

failure_patterns.pattern: Confusing program completion date with graduation ceremony date when calculating the 60-day grace period
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Universities prominently advertise graduation ceremonies; the academic completion date buried in registrar records is less visible and emotionally less salient
failure_patterns.consequence: Student believes they have weeks or months more than they actually do; accrues unlawful presence without realizing it, triggering 3-year or 10-year bars on re-entry
warning_signs: Student references graduation ceremony date when asked about their completion date
Student has not confirmed completion date with registrar or DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying for OPT on or after graduation day instead of 60–90 days before
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat OPT as something to do 'after' graduation; they underestimate USCIS processing time and overestimate how quickly they need authorization
failure_patterns.consequence: Multi-month work authorization gap; job offers rescinded or deferred; unemployment days accumulate before any work is possible
warning_signs: Student plans to 'celebrate graduation first then deal with OPT paperwork'
Student has not contacted DSO to initiate SEVIS authorization

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to track cumulative unemployment days on OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally anchor to the 90-day number as large and safe; they do not account for compounding gaps — USCIS delays, job search time, onboarding gaps — that all draw from the same pool
failure_patterns.consequence: Automatic OPT termination and immediate out-of-status; no reinstatement mechanism; forced departure or status violation
warning_signs: Student cannot state how many unemployment days they have used when asked
Student is on second job search without having tracked days from first gap

failure_patterns.pattern: Traveling internationally during OPT without obtaining an updated I-20 travel signature
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume that if their visa stamp and EAD are valid, re-entry is guaranteed; the I-20 travel endorsement requirement is not widely understood and is easy to overlook during travel planning
failure_patterns.consequence: CBP denies re-entry at port of entry; student stranded abroad; possible OPT abandonment and need to restart immigration process
warning_signs: Student books international travel without scheduling a DSO appointment first
Student's last I-20 travel signature is more than 6 months old

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming OPT transfers automatically to a new school when transferring SEVIS
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate SEVIS record transfer (administrative) with OPT authorization (school-specific legal status); the distinction is not intuitively obvious
failure_patterns.consequence: Work authorization terminates the moment transfer release date is set; student is immediately unauthorized to work without recourse
warning_signs: Student says they plan to 'transfer their OPT' to the new school
Student initiates transfer in SEVIS without consulting DSO about OPT timing

failure_patterns.pattern: DSO-triggered SEVIS termination due to unreported address change or missed registration
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on employment and visa milestones while treating administrative compliance as low-priority bureaucracy; 15-day address reporting window is short and easily missed during moves
failure_patterns.consequence: DSO must terminate SEVIS record; student falls out of F-1 status; all downstream OPT and work authorization is voided
warning_signs: Student moved apartments and has not updated address with DSO within 15 days
Student deferred registration without formal DSO approval

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the exact date your academic requirements were fulfilled — not your graduation ceremony date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student understands when their 60-day grace period actually started and whether they may already be accruing unlawful presence
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you confirmed this date with your registrar and DSO, and have you already filed or initiated your OPT application?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Contact your registrar immediately to get the official program completion date before making any other immigration decision

diagnostic_questions.question: How many cumulative unemployment days have you used since your OPT start date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of automatic OPT termination and whether they have enough buffer to conduct a job search or change employers
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If under 60 days used, proceed with job search but set a hard deadline to secure employment before day 80; if over 75 days, escalate to DSO immediately
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If student cannot answer, they are not tracking — treat as high risk and reconstruct the count from employment records immediately

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your current or prospective employer enrolled in E-Verify?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is eligible for STEM OPT extension, which determines if they have 12 or 36 total months of OPT work authorization
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm STEM degree CIP code eligibility and begin STEM extension application before current OPT expires
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Assess whether employer can enroll in E-Verify; if not, evaluate whether a different employer or freelance arrangement is possible before OPT expires

diagnostic_questions.question: When was the travel signature on your I-20 last endorsed by your DSO?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student can legally re-enter the US after international travel without risk of CBP denial
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If within 6 months, travel is permissible; remind student to also carry EAD and employment letter
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If more than 6 months ago or unsigned, student must visit DSO for endorsement before any international departure

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you planning to transfer to a new school while your OPT is still active?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is about to unknowingly terminate their work authorization by setting a SEVIS transfer release date
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Advise student to delay setting the transfer release date until after OPT end date; connect them with DSO to coordinate timing
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Confirm student understands the rule for future planning; if they are considering future enrollment, document the OPT end date for reference

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you used OPT at this degree level before?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has already exhausted OPT eligibility at their current degree level and whether a new degree enrollment could unlock additional eligibility
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Explore whether enrollment in a higher degree level is academically and strategically viable to access a fresh 12-month OPT
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Student retains full OPT eligibility at current degree level; ensure application timing is optimized

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you reported your current address to your DSO within the past 15 days of any move?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of a DSO-triggered SEVIS termination due to administrative non-compliance
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm records are current; no further action needed on this dimension
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Student must update address with DSO immediately; failure to do so is a mandatory SEVIS termination trigger

industry_realities.common_belief: The 60-day grace period after graduation is a safety buffer that gives you time to figure out your next steps
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 60-day grace period is a hard-deadline transition window; it begins at academic completion (not ceremony), and every day of inaction in it consumes irreplaceable time — there is no extension
industry_realities.implication: Students must initiate OPT applications or status change decisions before graduation, not after; treating the grace period as buffer almost guarantees procedural failure

industry_realities.common_belief: OPT belongs to the student and travels with them when they transfer to a new school
industry_realities.actual_reality: OPT is school-specific and is legally tied to the authorizing institution's SEVIS record; it terminates automatically and immediately when a SEVIS transfer release date is set — no exceptions
industry_realities.implication: Any student on OPT who is considering school transfer must delay the SEVIS transfer release until after OPT ends or they will lose work authorization with no recovery option

industry_realities.common_belief: 90 unemployment days is a generous window to find a new job during OPT
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 90 days is a cumulative lifetime limit across the entire OPT period, not a per-job-search reset; USCIS processing delays, gaps between jobs, and onboarding delays all count against it simultaneously
industry_realities.implication: Students must treat each day without an active employer on record as a resource expenditure, maintain a running count, and treat approaching 75 days as an emergency threshold

industry_realities.common_belief: As long as your visa and EAD are valid, you can re-enter the US after international travel
industry_realities.actual_reality: CBP requires a valid passport, unexpired F-1 visa stamp, I-20 with a travel signature dated within the past 6 months, EAD card, and an employment letter — any missing document can result in re-entry denial
industry_realities.implication: International travel during OPT must be planned with a DSO appointment as a mandatory pre-departure step; last-minute travel without updated I-20 endorsement is high risk

industry_realities.common_belief: Graduation ceremony date and program completion date are the same thing
industry_realities.actual_reality: Graduation ceremonies are often held months after the actual academic completion date; the immigration clock starts at academic completion, which is often in December for spring ceremony graduates
industry_realities.implication: Students must obtain their official academic completion date from the registrar and use that — not the ceremony date — as the basis for all immigration timeline calculations

industry_realities.common_belief: You can enroll in a new degree program at the same level to get a fresh OPT authorization
industry_realities.actual_reality: OPT eligibility resets only when the new degree is at a different level (e.g., completing a bachelor's and then completing a master's); same-level degrees do not grant a new OPT period
industry_realities.implication: Students who plan to use additional degrees as an immigration bridge must ensure the new degree is at a higher academic level than the one that already generated OPT

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats the 60-day grace period as vacation time after graduation; plans to 'start the OPT process' after the ceremony and take a few weeks off
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Applies for OPT 60–90 days before academic completion so the EAD arrives close to graduation; understands the grace period is a procedural transition window, not free time
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who wait create a multi-month unauthorized work gap and risk accruing unlawful presence if they misidentify the start of the grace period

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Knows vaguely that there is a '90-day limit somewhere' but cannot state how many days they have used; assumes they have plenty left
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintains a running log of every day without an active employer from OPT start date; treats day 75 as a hard alarm threshold requiring immediate escalation to DSO
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Exceeding 90 days causes automatic, unrecoverable OPT termination; students who are not counting are flying blind toward a cliff

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Checks that their visa and EAD are valid before booking international travel; assumes that is sufficient for re-entry
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Builds a pre-departure checklist: valid passport, valid F-1 visa stamp, I-20 with travel signature within 6 months, EAD, employment letter; schedules DSO appointment before every international trip
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Missing the I-20 travel signature is the most common cause of CBP re-entry denial for OPT students; a valid visa and EAD do not substitute for it

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Believes OPT can be 'transferred' to a new school the same way a SEVIS record is transferred; plans to transfer mid-OPT without consulting DSO
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows OPT is school-specific and non-portable; strategically times SEVIS transfer release date to occur after OPT expiration to preserve work authorization
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Setting the transfer release date while OPT is active immediately and permanently terminates work authorization — the error cannot be reversed

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Uses graduation ceremony date for all immigration planning; has not verified actual academic completion date with registrar
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Requests official program completion date from registrar at the beginning of final semester; anchors all OPT application timing and grace period calculations to that date
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Using the wrong date by even a few months can cause a student to unknowingly accrue unlawful presence, triggering multi-year re-entry bars

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Strategic use of degree levels for OPT eligibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes OPT is a one-time benefit that expires after graduation; does not know that each degree level independently generates OPT eligibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maps out OPT eligibility across all degree levels already completed and not yet completed; incorporates degree-level strategy into long-term immigration planning alongside H-1B lottery timelines
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Strategic enrollment at a new degree level can legally extend total US work authorization by years, buying additional H-1B lottery chances or time to pursue alternative immigration pathways

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Plans to 'look into the STEM extension' after standard OPT expires; has not confirmed employer E-Verify status or prepared I-983
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Verifies employer E-Verify enrollment on day one of employment; prepares I-983 Training Plan with employer months before standard OPT expires; files extension while current EAD is still valid
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Filing after OPT expiration means work authorization has already lapsed; early preparation ensures continuity of authorized employment and maximizes total OPT time available
